ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for wholemeal bread with kaya and butter as a composite Singaporean dish; mapped to the broader bread food product class as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for wholemeal kaya toast; A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Singaporean breakfast dish.

serving.description: 2 slices of wholemeal bread with kaya and butter
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 80 g, equivalent to two slices of wholemeal bread each spread with kaya and butter, as commonly served at Singaporean kopitiams and hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Wholemeal bread with kaya and butter — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for wholemeal bread with kaya and butter (cr_id F05020141). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical source. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 8.5 g/100g based on known butter and kaya composition; trans fat estimated at 0.1 g/100g from butter contribution. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values not reported in HPB source and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Wholemeal bread GI — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to wholemeal bread with kaya and butter composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 69 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for wholemeal bread (medium GI category). The composite dish (wholemeal bread + kaya + butter) is expected to have a similar or slightly lower GI due to fat and protein from butter slowing gastric emptying; GI 69 is retained as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (69 × 36.62 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 20.2 for an 80 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) from butter exceeds the 5 g/100g arterial stiffness threshold; trace trans fat (0.1 g/100g) adds cardiovascular risk; sodium at 249 mg/100g is moderate; potassium is very low at 21 mg/100g, yielding an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high saturated fat content from butter and very low potassium; replacing butter with avocado spread and reducing kaya quantity would substantially improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (69) combined with sugar at 9.33 g/100g from kaya and saturated fat at ~8.5 g/100g from butter worsens insulin resistance; glycemic load per 80 g serving is approximately 20.2, which is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit this dish because the combination of medium-GI carbohydrates, high sugar from kaya, and saturated fat from butter produces a high glycemic load and impairs insulin sensitivity; substituting kaya with a small portion of nut butter and replacing butter with an unsaturated fat spread would reduce glycemic and metabolic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~8.5 g/100g and trans fat at 0.1 g/100g from butter raise LDL cholesterol and increase atherosclerotic risk; cholesterol content is 53.75 mg/100g; coconut-derived saturated fat in kaya further contributes to atherogenic lipid profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish due to its high saturated fat and trans fat content from butter and kaya; replacing butter with extra-virgin olive oil spread and using a reduced-sugar kaya would meaningfully lower the atherogenic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.1 g/100g) from butter is a hard pro-inflammatory trigger; saturated fat at ~8.5 g/100g from butter and coconut-derived fat in kaya both exceed the 5 g/100g RA threshold; no anti-inflammatory omega-3 or polyphenol content is present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish because even trace trans fat from butter is strongly pro-inflammatory; replacing butter with extra-virgin olive oil and kaya with avocado would convert this to a more anti-inflammatory breakfast option.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholemeal bread with kaya and butter is a classic Singaporean breakfast dish consisting of sliced wholemeal bread spread generously with kaya — a sweet, fragrant coconut and egg jam — and a layer of salted butter. It is a wholegrain variant of the iconic kaya toast, a staple of Singaporean kopitiams (traditional coffee shops) and hawker centres. The wholemeal bread provides dietary fibre (3.98 g/100g) and a medium glycemic index of approximately 69, while kaya contributes sugar (9.33 g/100g) and coconut-derived saturated fat, and butter adds further saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) and trace trans fat (0.1 g/100g). Per 100 g, the dish delivers 329 kcal, 8.97 g protein, 16.79 g fat, and 36.62 g carbohydrate. Sodium is moderate at 249 mg/100g. The dish is high in calcium (127 mg/100g) and iron (4.03 mg/100g) from the fortified wholemeal bread. Clinically, the combination of medium-GI carbohydrates, high saturated fat, and added sugar makes this dish unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without modification. It is best consumed occasionally and in small portions. Traditionally served as a breakfast or morning snack alongside kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea) and soft-boiled eggs seasoned with soy sauce and white pepper.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020141, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for wholemeal bread as the closest analogous approved source. Saturated fat and trans fat values are estimated from known butter and kaya composition and documented accordingly.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond calcium and iron not reported in HPB source for this composite dish. Saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) and trans fat (0.1 g/100g) are estimated from known butter composition (approximately 50–51% saturated fat of total fat contribution) and are consistent with the health suitability assessments in the input record. Halal status is marked false because standard butter may contain non-halal-certified dairy; a halal-certified version would require verified halal butter and kaya. Glycemic load per serving calculated for an 80 g serving: (69 × 36.62 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 20.2.
